sections.header: Verses 1–11 — The Ascension: You Will Be My Witnesses

heb.paragraph: the imperfect of archomai — "what Jesus began to do." Acts presents itself as the continuation of Luke's Gospel: the risen Lord's work through the Spirit and the church is the direct sequel to his earthly ministry. The word frames the entire book as unfinished action.
an aorist participle indicating the commission was fully given before the Ascension. The disciples carry a complete mandate forward, not a partial one.

places.name: Mount of Olives
places.text: The site of the Ascension — a Sabbath day's walk (c.1 km) east of Jerusalem. Jesus descended this same hill on Palm Sunday. Here the heavenly departure takes place, and angels promise a return in like manner (v11).

places.name: Jerusalem
places.text: The geographic anchor of Acts 1–7. The disciples are commanded to wait here until Pentecost. The city where the resurrection occurred is the required starting point for witness that spreads outward.

tl.name: The Ascension
tl.text: Forty days after the resurrection, Jesus ascends from the Mount of Olives. The disciples return to Jerusalem to await the promised Spirit.

refs.note: The resurrection commission and the promise to wait in Jerusalem — Acts 1 resumes exactly at Luke's conclusion.
The closing scene of Acts has Paul proclaiming the kingdom of God — the same phrase as 1:3, forming the book's theological inclusio.
The cloud that receives Jesus at the Ascension (v9) connects to the Son of Man coming with the clouds — Jesus's exaltation to the Ancient of Days.

notes.note: The command "Do not leave Jerusalem" is strategically important: the Spirit was to come publicly, in the city of the pilgrimage festivals, where Diaspora Jews would witness the event and carry the news home. The geographic concentration was temporary and deliberate.
The "power" of the Spirit is not primarily spiritual gifts but effective witness-bearing. The commission is not optional or limited to the Twelve — it is the charter of the entire church.

The cloud that receives Jesus is the Shekinah glory cloud. The angels' promise that he "will come back in the same way" guarantees a literal, physical, visible return — not a spiritual or metaphorical one.
The command to wait is a test of obedience: the disciples must resist the impulse to begin their mission before they are equipped. The gift is promised; patience must precede possession.

The disciples' question about restoring the kingdom to Israel reveals how earthly their expectations still were. Jesus redirects: the kingdom they should concern themselves with is not national restoration but the advance of the Gospel worldwide.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The question uses apokathistanō — the standard LXX word for eschatological restoration (Jer 16:15; Ezek 16:55). The disciples are not simply confused; they are asking a genuine OT-grounded question. Jesus's answer reframes but does not dismiss it.
"In the same way" (hon tropon) guarantees the mode of return matches the mode of departure: bodily, visibly, from the sky. It rules out purely spiritual interpretations of the Second Coming.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: Luke opens with "my former book" (protos logos) — the technical term in ancient epistolary style for the first of a series. The perfect tense implies the Gospel remains valid in Theophilus's possession and is supplemented, not superseded, by Acts.

"You will receive power when the Holy Spirit comes on you" — the passive construction with the Spirit as agent is deliberate: believers are acted upon, not the initiators. The "power" (dynamis) is specifically for witness-bearing, not primarily for sign-gifts.

keener.source: Craig S. Keener, Acts: An Exegetical Commentary (4 vols., 2012–2015) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Luke's phrase "many convincing proofs" (tekmēriois) is the strongest Greek term for evidence, used in legal and philosophical contexts for irrefutable demonstration. This is apologetic historiography: Luke establishes the resurrection's factual basis before narrating its consequences.

"To the ends of the earth" (heos eschatou tēs gēs) draws from Isaiah 49:6, where it describes the reach of the Servant of the Lord. By placing this in Jesus's commission, Luke ties the church's mission to Israel's own prophetic vocation.

hist.context: Acts is the second volume of Luke-Acts — the longest single work in the NT. Luke's prologue (Luke 1:1-4) promises an 'orderly account' for Theophilus; Acts 1:1 resumes that project. The commission of v8 ('Jerusalem... Judea and Samaria... ends of the earth') is the book's own structural outline: Acts 1–7 = Jerusalem; 8–12 = Judea and Samaria; 13–28 = ends of the earth. Luke places the entire plot summary in the mouth of Jesus himself.

sections.header: Verses 12—26 — Waiting in Jerusalem; Matthias Chosen

heb.paragraph: Aramaic ḥaqal dema. Luke preserves the transliteration as historical evidence — a verifiable Jerusalem place-name still known to the community when Acts was written.

the last use of the OT lot-casting method in the NT. After Pentecost, the Spirit guides through spiritual discernment (Acts 13:2; 15:28). This is a transitional moment: the old and new eras overlap in Acts 1.

places.name: Upper Room
places.text: The room (hyperōon) where the disciples gathered — most likely the same large upper room as the Last Supper (Luke 22:12). The first gathering place of the Christian community, in the upper city of Jerusalem.

places.name: Field of Blood (Akeldama)
places.text: Located south of Jerusalem in the Hinnom Valley, identifiable since Byzantine times. The field purchased with Judas's betrayal money became a permanent marker in Jerusalem's geography.

tl.name: Matthias Chosen
tl.text: Before Pentecost, the eleven pray and cast lots, restoring the Twelve. The number mirrors the twelve tribes — the new community is constituted as reconstituted Israel from its founding moment.

refs.note: Cited in v20 for the vacant place — "May his place be deserted." Peter reads the psalm's language about the righteous sufferer's enemies as prophetically applicable to the one who betrayed the Righteous One.
Cited for the replacement — "May another take his place of leadership." Both psalms are read as requiring historical fulfilment, demonstrating the apostolic hermeneutic of prophetic necessity.
The church is "built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets." The restoration of the Twelve preserves the foundation's completeness before the Spirit comes.

notes.note: The 120 devoted themselves to prayer before the Spirit came — not to program or strategy. The church's first corporate act is not outreach but waiting before God. Order matters: prayer precedes power.

The apostolic qualifications — from John's baptism through the resurrection — are about eyewitness standing, not spiritual maturity. The apostolic office requires direct encounter with the risen Christ, which is why Paul's apostleship required the Damascus road appearance (1 Cor 9:1; 15:8–9).
The casting of lots was preceded by prayer and is presented positively. Paul's apostleship was of a different, unique order — the stronger case is that Matthias was legitimately chosen.

Peter's address citing the Holy Spirit's authorship of Scripture demonstrates that apostolic authority is always derivative — Peter shows that Scripture has already made the decision. This is the pattern of all true church governance: obedience to the written word.

The casting of lots was appropriate for this unique transitional situation and is not a general model. After Pentecost, the Spirit provides sufficient guidance through the word and prayer. The point is not the method but the prayerful submission to God's sovereign choice.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The apparent discrepancy between Matthew's account (priests bought the field) and Luke's (Judas bought it) is harmonised: the field was legally associated with Judas because his money funded the purchase, even if the priests were the direct buyers.

The verb "was counted" (synkatepsēphisthē) is a rare compound used only here in the NT — meaning to vote or reckon together with. Luke uses precise civic vocabulary, reflecting his awareness that he is recording events with historical and legal significance.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: "Brothers and sisters" (adelphoi) in v15 is generic — the mixed group of men and women of v14. The 120 represents a cross-section: apostles, women, and Jesus's formerly skeptical brothers.

The lot (klēros) is the last recorded use of this OT method in the NT. After Pentecost, the Spirit provides guidance through the word and prayer. Robertson sees Acts 1 as a deliberate transitional moment between eras.

keener.source: Craig S. Keener, Acts: An Exegetical Commentary (4 vols., 2012–2015) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The brothers' presence confirms the resurrection converted them. James becomes the leader of the Jerusalem church (Acts 15:13; Gal 1:19) — a remarkable trajectory from unbelief to martyrdom.

Casting lots was a well-attested Jewish method for discerning divine will (Lev 16:8; Prov 16:33; also used at Qumran). The preceding prayer (vv.24–25) distinguishes it from mere chance: God is asked to guide the outcome.

hist.context: The Matthias election (vv.15–26) is the last OT-era decision of the church: lots cast after prayer, using Psalms 69 and 109 as interpretive keys. The apostolic qualification — eyewitness of the resurrection from John's baptism onward — defines the unique, unrepeatable foundation of the church's testimony (cf. Eph 2:20). The presence of Mary and Jesus's brothers in the upper room (v14) is significant: the brothers had been unbelievers during Jesus's ministry (John 7:5). The resurrection has converted them. James will become leader of the Jerusalem church (Acts 15:13).

ppl.name: Paul↗ People
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Peter↗ People
ppl.role: Lead apostle; spokesperson for the Jerusalem church
ppl.text: Peter (Simon bar Jonah) dominates Acts 1–12 as the church’s primary spokesman and miracle-worker. He preaches at Pentecost (Acts 2), heals at the Beautiful Gate (Acts 3), defends before the Sanhedrin (Acts 4–5), raises Tabitha (Acts 9:36–42), and receives the vision that opens the gospel to Gentiles (Acts 10). His release from Herod’s prison by an angel (Acts 12) closes his narrative in Acts; Paul’s story takes over from ch.13.

ppl.name: Barnabas↗ People
ppl.role: Paul’s first missionary partner; 'Son of Encouragement'
ppl.text: Barnabas (Joseph of Cyprus) is introduced as a generous land-seller in Acts 4:36–37 and is the one who vouches for the newly converted Paul to the suspicious Jerusalem church (Acts 9:27). He leads the Antioch church (Acts 11:22–24) and takes Paul as his partner for the first missionary journey (Acts 13–14). Their sharp disagreement over Mark (Acts 15:36–40) ends their partnership but doubles the missionary coverage.

ppl.name: Stephen↗ People
ppl.role: First martyr; deacon whose speech reframed salvation history
ppl.text: Stephen is appointed as one of the seven deacons in Acts 6 to serve the Hellenistic widows. His speech before the Sanhedrin (Acts 7) is the longest speech in Acts and a sweeping reinterpretation of Israel’s history as a pattern of rejecting God’s messengers. He is stoned while seeing the Son of Man standing at God’s right hand. His death scatters the Jerusalem church and launches the Gentile mission (Acts 8:1–4).

ppl.name: Philip↗ People
ppl.role: Evangelist; deacon who brought the gospel to Samaria and Ethiopia
ppl.text: Philip the evangelist (distinct from Philip the apostle) is one of the seven deacons of Acts 6. Scattered by Stephen’s persecution, he preaches in Samaria with signs and wonders (Acts 8:4–13), then is directed by an angel to the Ethiopian eunuch on the Gaza road (Acts 8:26–40), baptising him and inaugurating the gospel’s African reach. He later hosts Paul at Caesarea (Acts 21:8).

ppl.name: James↗ People
ppl.role: Brother of Jesus; leader of the Jerusalem church
ppl.text: James the brother of Jesus (not the apostle James son of Zebedee) emerges in Acts as the dominant figure of the Jerusalem church. He is not mentioned in Acts 1–9 but appears decisively in Acts 12:17 (Peter reports to him after his prison release), acts as president at the Jerusalem Council (Acts 15:13–21), and receives Paul’s final Jerusalem report (Acts 21:18). Paul calls him a "pillar" of the church (Gal 2:9). He authored the letter of James.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVHe said to them: \ESVHe said to them, “It is not for you to know times or seasons that the Father has fixed by his own authority.NIVOn one occasion, while he was eating with them, he gave them this command: \ESVAnd while staying with them he ordered them not to depart from Jerusalem, but to wait for the promise of the Father, which, he said, “you heard from me;

src.title: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews (c.93 AD)
src.quote: Josephus provides extensive first-century background for the political, religious, and social conditions described in Acts — the Herodian family, Pharisees and Sadducees, Roman provincial governance.
src.note: Acts’ historical details consistently align with Josephus’s independent account of the same period, supporting the narrative’s historical credibility.

src.title: Dead Sea Scrolls (1QS, 1QM)
src.quote: The Qumran community texts illuminate the diversity of Second Temple Judaism from which Christianity emerged — their eschatological expectations, community rules, and temple criticism parallel aspects of the early church.
src.note: The Scrolls demonstrate that the early church’s practices (community meals, shared goods, eschatological expectation) were not unprecedented within Jewish renewal movements.

src.title: Greco-Roman Travel Literature and Epistolary Conventions
src.quote: Acts’ travel narrative (ch.13–28) conforms to the conventions of ancient Greek travel literature, and Paul’s speeches follow rhetorical models documented in Greco-Roman oratory.
src.note: The Hellenistic literary conventions confirm Acts’ composition for a sophisticated Greco-Roman audience and support its historical reliability as ancient historiography.

rec.who: John Chrysostom, Homilies on Acts (c.400 AD)
rec.text: "Chrysostom’s 55 homilies on Acts are the most thorough patristic commentary, emphasising the Spirit’s active role and the apostles’ moral example for the church."

"The command "Do not leave Jerusalem" is strategically important: the Spirit was to come publicly, in the city of the pilgrimage festivals, where Diaspora Jews would witness the event and carry the new"

rows.label: Verses 12—26 — Waiting in Jerusalem; Matthias Chosen
rows.text: Context  The Matthias election (vv.15–26) is the last OT-era decision of the church: lots cast after prayer, using Psalm…

pairs.label: A
pairs.top: Peter’s Pentecost sermon: the Spirit falls on the Jews in Jerusalem. Three thousand are added. Peter explains what God is doing through Joel and the Psalms (chs. 1–2)
pairs.bottom: Paul’s Pisidian Antioch sermon: the gospel goes to the Gentiles. Paul explains what God is doing through the Psalms and the prophets. The word spreads through the whole region (ch. 13)

pairs.label: B
pairs.top: Peter heals a lame man at the temple gate. The healing provokes a sermon and opposition from the Sanhedrin (chs. 3–4)
pairs.bottom: Paul heals a lame man at Lystra. The healing provokes a response — but pagan worship instead of Jewish opposition. Paul is stoned (ch. 14)

pairs.label: C
pairs.top: Peter’s shadow heals the sick; apostles arrested, beaten, released with joy. The gospel cannot be stopped by the religious establishment (ch. 5)
pairs.bottom: Paul and Barnabas return to Antioch, reporting that God opened a door of faith for the Gentiles. The Jerusalem Council meets to resolve the question of Gentile inclusion (ch. 15)

center.label: X
center.text: The conversion of Cornelius (chs. 10–11) and the surrounding narrative of the gospel crossing boundaries: Philip and the Ethiopian eunuch, Saul’s conversion on the Damascus road, the Antioch church (chs. 8–12). The center of Acts’ first half is the theological crisis that drives the entire book: God is doing something new with the Gentiles, and the church must decide whether to follow. Peter’s vision of the unclean animals and Cornelius’s household baptism are the pivot.

tx.title: Western text (Codex Bezae D) vs Alexandrian text
tx.content: Alexandrian(P45, P74, א, B) is the primary scholarly text.Western(Codex Bezae D) is 10–15% longer, with additions that appear to expand and harmonise.
tx.note: Acts has the most significant NT textual variation. The Western text of Acts is not simply corrupt but may preserve early oral expansions. Most scholars follow the shorter Alexandrian text as more original.

tx.title: P45 and early papyrus witnesses
tx.content: The Chester Beatty Papyrus (P45, c.250 AD) is the earliest substantial Acts manuscript, generally supporting the Alexandrian text with some unique readings.
tx.note: The papyrus evidence has largely confirmed the Alexandrian tradition as the best text of Acts, though the Western text’s substantial additions remain a subject of scholarly investigation.

debate.title: Authorship and Historicity of Scripture Walkthrough

positions.name: Traditional Lukan authorship (Hengel, Keener, classical scholarship)
positions.proponents: Attributes Acts to Luke the physician (Col 4:14), a companion of Paul, on the basis of the "we" passages, historical accuracy, and early church tradition.
positions.argument: Luke’s medical vocabulary (noted by Hobart) and the detailed geographical and political accuracy of Acts support the traditional attribution.

positions.name: Anonymous/secondary authorship (Haenchen, Conzelmann)
positions.proponents: Questions Lukan authorship on the basis of discrepancies between Acts’ Paul and the Pauline letters, suggesting a later author using Lukan sources.
positions.argument: This view notes that Acts does not always match Paul’s letters precisely, and argues the "we" passages may be a literary device rather than eyewitness markers.

coaching.tip: Acts opens where Luke's Gospel ended — with the risen Jesus and the disciples. The question 'Lord, will you at this time restore the kingdom to Israel?' (v. 6) shows they still expect a political Messiah. Jesus's answer redirects: not times and seasons but power and witness. The kingdom comes differently than they imagine.

'You will be my witnesses in Jerusalem, in all Judea and Samaria, and to the ends of the earth' (v. 8) — this verse is the book's table of contents. Acts 1-7 covers Jerusalem, 8-12 Judea and Samaria, 13-28 the Gentile mission reaching Rome. Luke tells us upfront where the story is going. The Spirit provides the power; geography marks the progress.
